the road where Halsa calls the Holm down to name its own wrong

the revenue riding-officer

predicates.description: who holds the object at this scene (one holder at a time)
the load-bearing wreck-night truth a subject is the cause of
where a load-bearing object stands, as a state that changes when it is found or moved
the state of a light — burning, out, or shown to have been doused by a hand

facts.claim: On the wreck-night the tide-bell did not ring the turn of the tide as it always had.
On the wreck-night the Holm light, which marks the channel and the bar, was dark.
The light is out; she has never once in her life seen it fail and cannot say why it failed.
On the wreck-night Orne Veck stood in the lee of the chapel wall with a thing under his coat against his ribs that he meant no living soul should ever see.
Orne knew the Holm light was dark and knew the hour.
The Holm light had been made dark, and Orne meant that no one on the Holm should ever learn it had been made dark.
Out on the channel a hull found the Sneck, with the long grinding of timber and sea.
Orne had carried the lantern to the Ness that night and shown what he had shown, then stood in the chapel's lee and let the sea do the rest.

object.value: carried to the Ness and shown

facts.claim: Bryde the keeper saw the light go out from below, as if a hand had closed over it, and climbed the stair in the dark to relight it.
On the wet fourth turn of the stair Bryde's foot went and Bryde fell hard against the stone, broke the leg, and could not climb or call, the light still dark above.
By grey morning the shore was a ruin of broken ship; the Sneck had taken a vessel in the night.
The wrecked vessel was the Marran.
The Drang, the Holm's one road to the mainland at low water, was drowned under the high tide; until the ebb bared it, no one could leave or come, and the Holm was shut in.
The Sneck is a wicked piece of water; honest weather, a black night, and no light to steer by took the ship, and there will be men drowned under the wreckage.
No soul leaves the Holm till the ebb bares the road; whatever there is to find is here and cannot get off.
Bryde lay abed in the keeper's room with the leg splinted between two oar-blades, and could not be moved.
Halsa is to keep the tally while Bryde is down, for someone must keep the Holm's account.
Halsa took the keeper's tally-book, the ledger logging every tide, lamp-filling, and barrel landed in Bryde's hand.
The last line of the tally, in Bryde's own hand, reads 'Light out' with an hour beside it, and that hour is the same as the hour the Marran struck.
I did not douse the Holm light; write that, and I will say nothing else of how it came to be out.
The boy Wick came up from the shore at a run, white in the face.
I found a drowned man on the bar.
No — it was a cask, washed up.
It was only a lantern; I don't know which it was. Leave me be.
Wick is lying; he found a real thing, has it hidden in the loft under the straw, and is so feared of it he can't keep one telling in his mouth.
Something the boy found frightens him into lying; under three tellings there is a real thing and a fright, though she does not yet know what.
The officer set up his count above the tide-line and tallied every cask, bale, and crate the sea brought up out of the Marran.
A wreck is the Crown's business; what the sea throws up off a lost ship is accounted to the Crown, and I am the Crown on this rock until the cutter comes.
The officer did not yet know his count was short and had no way to know; he wrote down in good faith what the islanders carried and called to him.
My master is owed bonded brandy off this bottom, and I have the lading-bill to say how much; I'll have what's his to the cask when the count is done.
They pulled a living man from the surf, half-drowned, and carried him to the curing-shed; he gave his name as Cass Pellow, mate of the Marran.
I am the only living soul off her, I think.
We struck in the black, no light where the light should be; she broke her back and I was in the water.
I had hold of the supercargo, his coat in my fist, and the sea took him out of my hand into the dark and under; there was nothing a man could do.
She believed the mate's account — an honest, terrible thing, a ship lost to a black night and a drowned man the sea would not give back — and was sorry for him.
A woman passenger was brought off the wreck living with a sea-chest she would not let go of, and slept across it in the corner of the headman's house.
My name is Ysolt Marran — of the Marran.
The chest is mine and it is locked, and the mate is not to have it whatever he says or claims; keep that man from my chest.
The drowned supercargo was the passenger's own kin; she knew it and did not say it.
The supercargo had carried the chest's key on his body, and with it a paper that stood against the passenger and would unmake her claim.
Dunna Quick was first down at the ebb, before the officer was up and before the count was set, with her creel on her back.
Dunna carried up one cask of brandy and hid it snug behind her salt-sacks where no Crown pencil would find it, before the officer wrote his first line.
It is a plain gift of the sea, and what the sea gives the Holm keeps; it is real brandy, and we had nothing but salt fish since the spring.
The headman had bidden them, the night the timber came in, to take what the sea gives and say nothing to the Crown's man of what came up before he was counting.
It never crossed her mind that anything but weather had brought the Marran to the bar.
The heavy keel-stuff and spars have come up off the Ness end; a ship lost honest on the Sneck in that wind fetches up down-strand here, as fifty wrecks have before.
This one was steering; she had her head laid for a light out on the Ness, where no leading-mark belongs, and she steered to it.
The Crown's iron buoys took the channel and my living off me years ago, but I can still read what a light on the Ness would do: draw a vessel right onto the Sneck on a black night.
I have shown ships off the bar myself in the old days; but I will not name whose hand it might have been this time — I'll say only that a light was shown that should not have been.
A ship was steered to a wrong light; she does not yet know whose hand.
The headman called the Holm together in the chapel and spoke for the island, as he always had.

Weather and a bad bar take ships; we give the officer a plain account of a ship lost to the dark and a man drowned, what's ours by old right is ours, and we keep the Holm's own counsel as we always have.
Orne believed, as he spoke, that the boy had found only a drowned body on the bar; he did not know Wick had taken up the very lantern Orne had carried to the Ness and hidden it in the straw.
She watched the headman steer the whole Holm toward one telling only and asked herself for the first time why a man so careful would do so over a wreck the sea brought in honest.
The count is short against my bill: what's come ashore is one cask of brandy short of what the lading-bill says was aboard.
The sea doesn't drink brandy and a cask doesn't walk; some islander down at the ebb has had a cask away up the rocks — or the one man off her that lived, the mate, who'd know what she carried.

Let the factor blame the islanders and let them wrangle over a cask; it keeps every eye off the dead man's lading-bill hidden against my body and off the dead man's claim, which I mean to make my own.

The tally says the light was out at the hour she struck, the pilot says she steered to a false light on the Ness, the boy found something that frightens him witless, and Bryde will say only that he did not douse it.
When I started up the stair the light was already gone; the lamp had a full reservoir I filled myself at sundown and does not fail all at once — it went out at once, like a hand on it.
A hand doused that light, not the weather; I never saw the hand and cannot tell you whose, for it was black up there and I was down off the stair before I reached the lamp.
She believes Bryde now: the dark light was no accident, and the wreck on the bar and the doused light over it are not two matters but one.

A lantern, up high on the bar, dry as a bone, not wet at all, shuttered up; I never found a man — I said a man because a dry lantern is worse — and I hid it under the straw because I was feared, and have been feared ever since.
It is hidden under the straw by the third post; he'd not have hidden it if he'd not known it a wrong thing to find.
Halsa dug the lantern out of the straw with her own hands and held it to the loft window.

The lantern is not ship's gear but Holm-work, the local make; it was dry through, with burnt oil crusted on the wick and the reservoir near empty — it had been lit, lit long, burned till the oil was near gone, then shuttered, and left high on the bar where no wave touched it.

It was not weather: someone showed a false light off the Ness to draw the Marran onto the bar, doused the true light so she'd not be saved, and the starving Holm went down at the ebb to take what the lie washed up — one purpose, one hand, though she does not yet know the hand.
The factor's count is one cask short, so the Holm itself took from the wreck — Dunna's cask or another's, it comes to the same.
The ebb was making, the Drang baring, and the Crown cutter standing in toward the Sneck; by the next low water the Drang would be bare and the Holm open to the law again.

She has the false light, the doused light, the short cask, and the lantern: she knows the Marran was made to wreck and the Holm drank of it; but she does not yet know — and has no way to know — that the supercargo did not simply drown, that a hand held him under in the surf to take what he carried.

She is made to choose among three roads: carry it to the officer and let the Crown's law come down on the Holm; bury it and give the officer the plain wreck the headman wanted; or call the Holm itself down to the shore and make it name its own wrong before the law can.

Orne, too, watched the Drang begin to bare and reckoned the tide: a lantern he had thought safe under the sea was somewhere on his island, and he meant to have it back before the Crown could lay a hand on it, the cargo away, and the whole made thing buried under the face of an honest wreck.
Halsa carried the lantern and the keeper's tally to the officer's plank-table and set them on his ledger.

The lantern is Holm-work, not off the ship, found dry high on the bar; the keeper's tally reads Light out at the hour the Marran struck; the pilot will say she steered to a false light on the Ness; I think someone showed a false light to bring her onto the Sneck and doused our true light so she'd not be saved — the wreck was made.

You're telling me this wasn't weather but a made wreck — a hanging matter, and not one stranger's neck but the Holm's; if you're right I'll find it in the open before the cutter while everyone is shut on this rock.
I'm not sure, but sure enough to bring it to you and let you find out; I'll not be the Holm's law, I'll only not be its lie.
The officer wrote at the head of a clean page and the inquiry was begun.
The officer took the chest into Crown keeping over Ysolt's protest and, when she would not give the key, had it broken open with a bar on the headman's table.
The key is lost; it went down with the man who carried it.
The chest held no coin, plate, or treasure — only papers: a contested deed and inheritance papers, and a second deed standing against them.

This deed she sailed to lodge ashore; the deed standing against it was carried, I'd guess, by the man who drowned — a man who carried the one paper that could break your claim was worth more to someone dead than alive.
He was my own cousin; he carried the key and that filthy deed they'd got up against me, and was bringing it ashore to ruin me, and now he is drowned.
Hearing 'worth more dead than alive,' she for the first time lets herself think plainly that the supercargo's death might have been no honest drowning.

A light on the Ness draws a ship onto the Sneck; that is what was done to the Marran, a light was shown on the Ness. I did not show it — I had the knowing, having shown ships off this bar in the bad old days, but I did not light that light; my hands are clean of the Marran.
Then the false light is named to the Crown — shown from the Ness, Holm-work; the question left is whose hand carried it.

I'll not own a thing I never did; I know nothing of any light on the Ness. The keeper's old, the stair's wet, the night was wild, and a lamp can fail — I'll lay our dark light to the weather and stand on that.
Orne had carried that very lantern to the Ness with his own hands, shown the false light, and doused the true one; faced with the proof on the table he would not own one grain of it.

object.value: showed the false light and doused the true, making the Marran wreck

facts.claim: I had the one cask, carried up at the ebb before any count — but the headman bid us take what the sea gives. I never lit a light, never knew of a light, I thought it the sea's plain gift; I'll give back the cask but I'll not hang for his wreck.
Dunna gave up the short cask to the Crown rather than carry the headman's wreck on her own neck.

Then it wasn't the mate — it was the island; the Holm lifted my master's cask with the headman's word behind it, and I'd have hanged that mate for it. The cask's recovered and the count's made right, but it's a made wreck, and I came for brandy and go home with a hanging.
Cass Pellow ran for the bared Drang at the ebb, toward the mainland and the cutter, with the dead supercargo's lading-bill buttoned inside his shirt.
He's running — the mate, running the Drang! Stop him!
The boy's cry brought the Holm down onto the shore; the mate faltered on the narrow road with water either side and was taken.
Halsa drew the sea-stained lading-bill, the dead man's, out of the mate's shirt.

He did not lose the supercargo to the sea; a man does not get a paper off a drowning kinsman's body by grieving — the supercargo was drowned by a hand in the surf for the thing he carried, and 'right out of my hand, into the dark' was the lie.
You held him under.
The mate said nothing, and the not-saying was enough.
You stay — you found the lantern and told it true, that's your part and it's done; you don't go down on the bar with a tide on the make to lay hands on a murdering man. We hold the loft.
The cutter came over the bared Drang on the dead low water, and the officer gave up the mate for the supercargo's death and stolen bill, and the headman for the false light and the made wreck.
Halsa watched them take Orne Veck down to the cutter's boat and did not look away.
It's the law's now, not mine; I only wouldn't be its lie.

I'll pilot the Crown's cutter over the Sneck — I know this water when no buoy does, I'm clean of the Marran owned so before the Crown and believed; I held my tongue too long once and I'll not do it twice.
All week I thought it honest weather, and it was Orne — Orne, that married my own cousin, that I've known forty year; I'll salt what the sea spoiled and grieve the man taken, both.
The cutter went out over the Drang with the headman and the mate aboard and the pilot conning her off the bar; the tide came back over the Drang and shut the Holm in again.

The doused light's owned to the Crown — not buried, not laid on me, not gone into the dark; I climbed that stair to relight a light a hand put out and broke my leg for it, and I'm glad it's said out loud whatever it cost. Write it in the tally: the light was owned.
Ysolt Marran left on the cutter, her chest broken but her deed lodged safe in Crown hands and her claim sound, the death of her kin named for what it was.
He came to ruin me with that deed and I'd have hated him living, but not held under in the surf for a paper; no one deserves that. Your island did a hard thing, naming its own; I'll not forget it.
Every cask accounted, the short one back in the count, the books square; I came for brandy and watched a man hang himself with his own light. I'll tell my master the count came right.
I kept my neck — gave the cask back and told the officer true — but I'm the one who gave the headman up, and the Holm will mark me for it as long as there's a Holm to remember. A neck and no name.

Halsa kept the tally and trimmed the light, set the great lamp burning steady over the Sneck where the false one had drawn the Marran in; the tide-bell rang the turn, and the Holm — diminished, named, alive — went on.
We've always been shut, but we're not lying anymore; that's the whole of what I've done — I made us stop lying.

Halsa let the wreck stand as the sea's: she came with the lantern wrapped under her arm meaning to carry it to the officer, and did not, going instead to his plank-table with nothing but the keeper's tally open to a line copied fresh — 'Light failed in the gale. Keeper injured climbing to relight.'

The light failed in the gale; Bryde went up to relight it and fell and broke a leg — you've seen the leg — and the Marran struck in the dark of it; it's a hard chance, but it's the Sneck. I keep the tally and that's the tally.
The officer, never shown the dry lantern and not yet knowing the count was short, took the islander's straight word and wrote it down as a wreck lost honest to a black night, asking nothing further.
A loss to the weather and the Sneck; God knows the Sneck's earned the reputation.

Failed. I climbed that stair and I know what I climbed for, but I'll keep the bed and my mouth if that's the road you've chosen; it's a heavy thing you've taken up, heavier than the leg — mind you can carry it.

You've the boy's lantern off the bar; give it me, Halsa — you've kept the Holm off the rope, don't leave the thing itself lying about for the next Crown man to trip over; give it me and the Holm's safe for good.
Halsa gave Orne the lantern.
That night Orne lifted a flag of the salt-house floor, dug into the cold sand, laid the lantern in, and trod the floor flat over it — the false light buried where the sea had failed to bury it.
Buried's not undone, Orne; it's only put where it can't be answered for.
Maon came on Orne in the salt-house dark with the spade still in his hand, and the old pilot held his silence and said no more of it to any living soul.

The Crown's written it weather, so there's no inquiry and no one breaks open my chest for evidence; I'll go ashore on your cutter with my chest unopened and my affairs my own, and in return I never carried a chest off this island and never saw a wreck that was anything but a wreck — my silence for my lock.
We have a bargain.

Halsa, who heard of the bargain after, let it stand: the passenger kept her chest closed and the deed unread, and the supercargo's death stayed an honest drowning; Halsa did not know, and never would on this road, what a hand had truly done in the surf.

With the wreck laid to weather and no inquiry, Cass kept the dead supercargo's lading-bill and the dead man's claim as his own, his hand never named by Crown or Holm; he had drowned a man for a paper and walked off the Sneck a free survivor, mourned for his ordeal.

object.value: drowned the supercargo in the surf for the lading-bill

facts.claim: The boy, unable to carry the knowing, went down white and shaking to the officer's door to tell that he found a dry lantern on the bar, the light was put out by a hand, and the ship was made to wreck.

No, Wick — not at that door; you'd be telling the rope where to come, for Dunna, for Orne, for us — you'd hang half the island and yourself with it. Telling it true at that door isn't brave, it's just loud. Come away.
Senna caught Wick at the very door and dragged him bodily from the officer's threshold.

I've made the choice for the Holm and it's mine to carry, not yours — but it's yours now too, and Senna's; God help me, I've put it on the children. You found a ship's lantern washed up, took it for kindling, and it's gone — that's the telling. Can you hold to it?
Dunna kept the short cask hidden behind her salt-sacks and her mouth shut; the count was never made right to the factor, the wreck being weather and no one counting twice.
The cutter came over the bared Drang and the officer carried off a plain wreck's report and nothing more.

A vessel lost on the Sneck in a black gale, her light failed her, keeper laid up with a broke leg trying to right it, one mate living, one passenger, the rest drowned — the sea's old work; nothing here for the Crown but a count and a burial.
The officer suspected nothing of any false light, nothing of the Ness, nothing of a hand in the dark; he had been given a clean grey wreck and believed it.

My count's short — one cask of my master's brandy, and I'll lay my life an islander's got it up the rocks; but you've written it weather, there's no inquiry, and I can't open every cot on my own say-so. Robbed, and no remedy; I'll go home short and tell my master the sea drank it.
Halsa watched the cutter take the truth away unseen, the Holm whole and shut and safe behind her, the light true again on its stair — and she was no longer clean of any of it.

Orne ruled the Holm as before, but it was different: the whole hamlet held his secret over him, every soul knowing what the headman was, and he ruled a place that would never again be quite his — he had bought the island's life with a lie and the island paid in silence.
Wick grew quiet and apart, kept to the high rocks and the loft, the lantern's truth sitting in him like a stone; he became a boy who kept one telling — the false one — and would keep it all his life.

Ysolt Marran did not leave: she stayed, her chest unopened, her claim unsettled, her deed unread and unlodged, bound to the Holm by the very bargain that saved her lock, a stranger settled among strangers and as shut in as the Holm.

It's a quiet island this year since the wreck; folk don't talk the way they did, as if they'd lost their own — and it was strangers drowned, an honest weather-loss like a dozen I've salted fish through. Strange, how a wreck of strangers can hush a whole island.

I'll not say it out, since you've chosen — but I'll write it all down true, the light doused by a hand, the hour, the false light the pilot read, the lantern the boy found, and keep it where no Crown finds it, against the day the Holm should need the truth and not the silence. A keeper keeps the true account, even the one no one's to read.
Bryde wrote the true account in the square careful hand and hid the true tally away.

Halsa kept the Holm whole and the light true, filling the lamp at sundown and setting it burning over the Sneck; the island lasted, but she carried the wreck-night's silence in her, the price of the only place she knew, with one drowned stranger's truth the coin gone into the cold sand under the salt-house floor.
Halsa called the whole Holm down to the bared shingle at the ebb, going hut to hut with the lantern wrapped under her arm; the whole shut island came, and last and unhurried the headman.

I'll not carry this to the Crown behind your backs, and I'll not bury it under a salt-house floor either; whatever was done the wreck-night was done by the Holm and to the Holm, and the Holm will hear it named by us, here, before the law comes — I'd rather we judged ourselves than have the Crown do it over our heads.
Orne came down to the front to put it down as he put everything in hand, not knowing the thing wrapped under Halsa's arm was the very lantern he had carried to the Ness; he came to quiet talk.

A light on the Ness draws a ship onto the Sneck; there's no mark out there, and a master in the dark takes a light for a leading-mark and she's on the bar before he knows the lie. The Marran steered to a light on the Ness — I read it in the lie of her timber; a light was shown out there the wreck-night, and I'm the one left who can read it.
Halsa unwound the sacking and held the dry Holm-work lantern up high over her head where the whole Holm could see it.

This is the false light — not off any ship but Holm-work, our own smith's hand, burned dry and carried out and left high on the bar where no wave touched it; this is the light shown from the Ness to bring the Marran onto the Sneck, from this island, by one of us.

I found it on the bar at first light, dry, shuttered; I hid it in the net-loft straw because I was feared and told it a man, told it a cask — it was that lantern; I've said it true and I'll not say it any way but true again.
Senna came out and stood at Wick's shoulder so he should not say it alone, her chin up and her hand at his back.

Aye — I carried it; I lit it on the Ness and doused our own light so she'd not be answered, and I drew her onto the Sneck. We were starving; another winter and there'd not have been a Holm to keep a light. I did it for the island, to bring it what the sea wouldn't give honest. It was never right, but it was mine and done for you, and I'll stand to it — the Marran's on the bar because of my hand and no weather.

object.value: lit the false light on the Ness and doused the Holm light, drawing the Marran onto the bar

facts.claim: Ysolt Marran came forward with her chest, set it on the wet shingle before them all, and opened it with a second key from a cord at her throat, cut against the loss of the first.

object.value: opened before the Holm on the bar

facts.claim: There is no coin, plate, or treasure — a deed, a contested deed and the papers of my inheritance I sailed to lodge ashore, and this, the deed they'd got up against me that would break my claim. The man who carried that paper against me and the key to this chest was my own kin, the supercargo — the man your mate says the sea took out of his hand. A man does not get a paper off a drowning kinsman's coat by grieving. Where is the key, mate? Where is the lading-bill?

The supercargo did not drown honest — you held him under in the surf in the dark till he was dead and took the bill off his body for what it was worth to you; that's why you alone came living off her, you made sure of it.

He'd have had it all, the cargo and the claim and every cask, and left me, the wreck's only living officer, nothing — so aye, I held him under in the surf; he went quiet and I took the bill off him and let the sea have the rest, and told you he slipped my hand. He slipped nothing. I drowned him for the paper.

object.value: held the supercargo under in the surf till dead and took the lading-bill

facts.claim: A killing — I came onto this rock to chase one short cask of brandy and I'd have hanged this man for that; my short cask is the least wrong done on this shore, the very least of it.
Dunna went up to her salt-house and carried the short cask back down, setting it among the recovered casks of the wreck before the factor and them all with her own two hands.

object.value: set back among the recovered wreck casks

facts.claim: I took it — first down at the ebb like always, and the headman bid us take what the sea gave, and I thought it the sea's plain gift; I never knew of any light, I'll swear that to God and the Crown both. There it is, set back, the count's whole by my own hand — count it.

This is ours to have named and we've named it — the headman's wreck and the mate's killing, owned on the Holm's own shore by the Holm's own people before ever the Crown set foot on the bar; we did it, not the cutter, us. Whatever the law does now, it does to men this island has already judged.

Forty year I held my tongue about the old trade, shown ships off this bar myself and never answered for it, and thought I'd carry the knowing into the ground; but it's named and owned, and it wasn't my hand this time — my hands are clean of the Marran, and the silence is done.

I climbed the stair to relight what his hand put out — the light was dark when I started up, the stair was wet, and I went down on the fourth turn and broke this leg; the doused light didn't only drown a ship, it broke your own keeper climbing in the dark to set right what Orne Veck put wrong. It's in the tally true, and now said out loud.
The officer came down last of all, drawn by the whole island gone to the bar, and found the inquiry he meant to open already held without him by its own subjects, on the bar at the ebb.

The Holm has judged its own before I could — the headman owns a made wreck, the mate owns a killing, and a count is made whole by the hand that broke it; I'll take the two of them for the cutter and the truth goes with them, but the truth was told here first, by you, and I'll set that down. The island named it. That's not nothing at all.

Halsa met the cutter at the tide-line with the officer and gave up the mate for the supercargo's death and the headman for the made wreck, but gave them up with the telling already told — the island's own, not wrung from a frightened people by the Crown.
Ysolt Marran went down to the boat with her deed shown and her chest open at last, her claim spoken before witnesses and her kin's death named on the bar where it happened.

On the mainland they'd have wrung this out over months in a Crown court and called it justice; you did it in a morning on a beach and called it the Holm looking to itself. I came off that wreck thinking I'd landed among thieves — I was wrong; I'll lodge my deed and not forget where it was first heard.

Made whole by the very woman who lifted it, and a wrecker and a murderer owned to my face before I could lay a wrong charge; I came for brandy and leave with a respect I didn't think to find on a starving rock. The island judged itself — I'll tell my master how, and he'll not believe the how.

I salted fish through a dozen wrecks on the Sneck and called every one the weather, and I'd have called this one the same to my dying day — and it was Orne, that I've known forty year. I'll never again call a wreck on this bar the sea's plain work without I think of this morning; the Holm named its own.
Wick kept the bar with Senna while the cutter pulled away; he did not hide his face this time, having told it true at the last before the whole Holm, and the stone was out of him.
Is it right, what we did?
It's true — that's better than right; right you can argue, true you can't.

Halsa watched the cutter take the headman and the mate away over the road; the island was whole — shut, shamed, grieving its headman, but whole and owned and not lying — for she had kept it together by making it stand on its own shore and name what it had done before the law could; the light burned true that evening, the tide-bell rang the turn, and the Holm lived on, having judged its own before the law could.
